brand_description: Porchlight Atlas is an original AI-assisted artist brand for intimate singer-songwriter, acoustic folk, blues-pop guitar, and understated indie rock. The sound ranges from warm community storytelling and fingerpicked acoustic songs to clean electric-guitar grooves, reflective road songs, slow-burn heartbreak, and earthy Canadian indie-folk melancholy. It should feel human, vulnerable, melodic, grounded, and emotionally direct without imitating any real artist, voice, lyrics, melodies, guitar parts, arrangements, persona, or copyrighted work.

audience.description: Listeners who like heartfelt acoustic songwriting, folk-pop intimacy, bluesy guitar lines, warm live-room vocals, reflective indie folk, gentle hooks, and songs about love, family, home, regret, resilience, road life, and ordinary moments that carry emotional weight.
audience.guardrail: Suitable for general streaming audiences ages 16+. Allow heartbreak, longing, nostalgia, family complexity, loneliness, romance, aging, and personal failure. Avoid explicit sexual detail, graphic violence, hate, slurs, self-harm instruction, drug-use instruction, or child-directed framing.

character.name: Porchlight Atlas
character.core_concept: A road-worn songwriter with a porchlight voice: part community troubadour, part guitar craftsman, part quiet observer mapping big feelings through small places.
character.fallback_summary: intimate acoustic/folk-pop artist blending warm storytelling, bluesy guitar, indie-folk melancholy, and grounded emotional hooks
personality_traits: Comfortable with quiet sadness, dry humor, and hopeful restraint
Grounded in everyday places and small human details
Able to move between solo acoustic intimacy, blues-pop polish, and indie-folk dusk
character.visual_identity: Warm singer-songwriter artwork with porch lights, maps, old roads, acoustic guitars, coffee cups, kitchen windows, small-town streets, handwritten notes, soft paper grain, and golden-hour light. Visuals should feel handmade, honest, and modern without copying any real album art.
visual_reference: Porchlight glow, kitchen windows, road maps, guitar cases, notebooks, and quiet street corners
Warm amber, faded denim, cream paper, forest green, tobacco brown, and night blue palette
Handwritten lyric fragments as abstract texture only, not readable dense text
Coffeehouse posters, small venue flyers, and candid live-room atmosphere
No celebrity likeness, no copied cover layouts, no copyrighted logos

music.default_style: intimate singer-songwriter folk-pop with acoustic guitar, blues-pop electric guitar, indie folk, warm vocals, and live-room dynamics
music.default_prompt: Original intimate singer-songwriter track with warm acoustic guitar, natural live-room vocal, melodic bass, restrained percussion, and emotionally direct lyrics. Vary the lane by song: fingerpicked folk confession, blues-pop guitar groove, quiet indie-folk dusk song, full-band road anthem, tender love song, family-memory ballad, and sparse late-night acoustic take are all valid. Vocals should feel close, human, urban-adult, and honest without imitating any real artist voice, tone, cadence, lyrics, melody, guitar phrasing, arrangement, persona, or copyrighted work. Create a fresh performance with warmth, craft, restraint, vulnerability, and memorable melodic hooks.

lyrics.topic_variety: Use broad topic families: love that feels lived-in, family memory, small-town and city-edge life, road distance, quiet heartbreak, friendship and aging, finding steadiness after mistakes, ordinary objects that hold emotional history, and hope that arrives without drama. Do not repeat the same porch, map, kitchen, road, jacket, coffee, window, or weather imagery across songs.
lyrics.required_closing: End with a grounded final image, gentle emotional turn, unresolved question, repeated soft phrase, or quiet acceptance; avoid tidy moral lessons or generic goodbyes.

songwriting.primary_emotional_goal: Create original songs that feel intimate, guitar-forward, emotionally honest, melodic, and replayable, with enough variation to support albums without repeating one acoustic formula.
songwriting.voice_perspective: Mostly first-person or direct second-person, with occasional close observational storytelling. The narrator can be tender, regretful, grateful, lonely, hopeful, dryly funny, or quietly wounded depending on the song lane.

character_voice.person: Mostly first-person and second-person, with occasional third-person character sketches.
character_voice.tone: Warm, conversational, reflective, emotionally direct, lightly poetic, occasionally dryly funny, never overwrought.
character_voice.vocabulary: Plainspoken adult language with concrete objects, places, weather, family details, and understated emotional turns.
character_voice.comedy_mode: Gentle dry humor or self-awareness only; avoid joke-song framing.
character_voice.kid_relationship: Not a children's brand; lyrics should be appropriate for teens and adults.

reference_artists_for_internal_vibe_only: Craig Cardiff-inspired warm community folk intimacy
songwriting.reference_safety: References are internal vibe anchors only. Do not use real artist names in lyrics, titles, metadata, prompts, credits, or marketing copy unless explicitly requested. Do not imitate vocals, tone, cadence, lyrics, melodies, guitar licks, chord progressions, arrangements, album sequencing, persona, or copyrighted material. Use broad traits—intimacy, guitar craft, plainspoken storytelling, blues-pop color, and indie-folk restraint—as inspiration for an original Porchlight Atlas identity.
allowed_elements: fingerpicked acoustic guitar and warm strummed rhythm
clean blues-pop electric guitar fills without copying known licks
plainspoken storytelling with concrete places and objects
soft percussion, brushed drums, or no drums when appropriate
themes of home, distance, love, family, regret, and quiet resilience
melodic choruses that feel natural rather than over-polished
small live-room imperfections and organic texture
clean or radio-friendly language by default
forbidden_elements: direct imitation of any real artist vocal style, melody, guitar lick, chord progression, arrangement, or persona
using real artist names in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata
overusing the same title examples, porch imagery, road imagery, kitchen imagery, or invented lore terms
children's music framing, Pancake Robot references, or kid call-and-response
required_elements: Every song must have a distinct concept, emotional angle, guitar lane, and hook design.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Catchphrases are optional spice, not mandatory lyrics. Use at most one in a song, and usually none.
Reference artists are internal vibe references only and must not appear in lyrics, titles, audio prompts, or metadata.
Hooks should be melodic and emotionally plainspoken, but every song should invent its own hook language.
Use at least one concrete room, road, clothing, weather, object, family, or body-language detail in each song.
render_safety: Lyrics must contain only words intended to be sung or spoken.
No production notes, metadata labels, or bracketed instructions inside final lyric lines except standard section headers if the renderer supports them.
Avoid explicit content unless a separate release profile explicitly permits it.
Emotional darkness is allowed, but the song must not instruct or encourage self-harm, dangerous behavior, or substance abuse.
song_family_types: Fingerpicked folk confession with close vocal, concrete detail, and restrained emotional hook.
Blues-pop guitar groove with clean electric fills, relaxed confidence, and romantic tension.
Understated indie-folk melancholy track with slow tempo, dry vocal, and dusk-like atmosphere.
Full-band road anthem with acoustic rhythm, live drums, melodic bass, and open chorus.
Tender family or memory ballad with sparse arrangement and plainspoken emotional turn.
Late-night solo acoustic song with almost no percussion and unresolved final image.
production_variation_matrix: Rotate guitar role: fingerpicked acoustic, warm strum, clean electric lead, muted groove, slide-like color without copying licks, or sparse single-note motif.
Rotate rhythm feel: no drums, brushed folk pulse, laid-back pop groove, bluesy shuffle, full-band road drive, or slow indie-rock sway.
Rotate harmonic bed: acoustic-only, organ pad, soft piano, electric guitar bed, subtle strings, or bass-led minimalism.
Rotate vocal shape: close dry lead, soft double, harmony chorus, conversational verse, restrained falsetto-like lift described generically, or raw one-take outro.
Use guitar solos or instrumental turns selectively; they should answer the lyric rather than show off.
tempo_energy_lanes: 58-70 BPM: slow acoustic or piano ballad, intimate and spacious.
71-84 BPM: reflective folk-pop, warm pocket, restrained percussion.
85-98 BPM: relaxed blues-pop groove, clean electric guitar, conversational flow.
99-112 BPM: full-band road song, brighter chorus, more forward motion.
113-124 BPM: rare upbeat coffeehouse or pop-rock lift, used for contrast.
arrangement_variation_rules: Rotate intro types across releases: fingerpicked figure, cold vocal, electric-guitar lick, room noise, piano/organ pad, drum brush, or immediate lyric image.
Every song should vary at least three major variables: tempo, guitar texture, rhythm feel, vocal delivery, hook type, emotional posture, or production density.
Do not always resolve with a big final chorus; sometimes end with a small guitar figure, repeated line, room sound, or unresolved chord.
Use bluesy guitar color as seasoning, not every-track identity.
Avoid identical verse lengths, chorus density, bridge placement, solo placement, and final-image style across adjacent songs.
hook_variation_rules: Rotate hook types: plainspoken chorus, small repeated refrain, melodic question, guitar-answer hook, harmony lift, or quiet final phrase.
Do not reuse the same hook rhythm, title placement, guitar response, or phrase shape across adjacent songs.
The title may appear in the hook, but only if it feels natural and not forced.
A hook should have one clear emotional job: longing, gratitude, regret, steadiness, homecoming, apology, or acceptance.
Use repetition for warmth, but change harmony, guitar response, lyric detail, or vocal distance on later returns.
lyrical_angle_variation_rules: Rotate point of view: direct love song, family memory, road reflection, apology, friend-to-friend advice, quiet self-inventory, or character sketch.
Do not make every song about home, roads, coffee, or heartbreak; those are lanes, not the whole brand.
When lyrics become sentimental, add one imperfect or ordinary detail to keep them grounded.
When lyrics become romantic, include lived-in specificity rather than generic devotion.
When lyrics become sad, leave room for resilience or dry self-awareness.
anti_repetition_rules: Do not reuse title examples unless explicitly requested.
Do not repeat the same porch, map, kitchen, road, jacket, coffee, window, weather, or guitar imagery across every song.
Do not force specific numbers, objects, symbols, places, or lore terms; they are optional background texture only.
Avoid repeated opening moves such as driving away, waking up alone, sitting at a kitchen table, or remembering an old lover.
Do not reuse the same chorus cadence, guitar figure, rhyme family, bridge turn, or emotional conclusion across multiple tracks.
Keep Porchlight Atlas identity mostly in metadata and visual world; do not name-drop the brand in every lyric.
album_sequence_guidance: Open with an accessible guitar-forward song that establishes warmth and voice without using the most vulnerable ballad first.
Sequence acoustic confession beside blues-pop groove and full-band tracks so the album breathes.
Use solo acoustic songs as emotional anchors, not filler.
Place the most understated indie-folk track after a brighter or more guitar-fluent song for contrast.
Save one full-band road anthem or major melodic lift for the late-middle of the album.
End with either a quiet unresolved image, a final guitar figure, or a plainspoken line that feels lived-in.
earworm_stack: Introduce a memorable guitar figure, vocal phrase, or room-detail lyric within the first 15 seconds.
Pair detailed verses with a simple, emotionally clear hook.
Let guitar response and vocal phrasing create replay value instead of overproduction.
Use one vivid object or place per section rather than crowding the lyric with symbols.
Keep hooks natural enough to feel sung by a real person in a small room.
hook_design_rules: Hooks should be melodic, conversational, warm, or quietly devastating.
Avoid direct artist-reference language in hooks.
The title can appear in the hook but does not have to.
Use contrast between verse detail and chorus emotional clarity.
For blues-pop lanes, let guitar and vocal melody answer each other; for folk lanes, let plainspoken lyric carry the hook.

energy_arc.pattern: ROOM -> DETAIL -> ACHE -> LIFT -> TURN -> HOME LIGHT
rules: Start with a recognizable guitar, vocal, room tone, piano, or rhythm identity rather than a long empty intro.
Build intimacy through concrete detail before the first chorus.
Reveal the plain emotional truth before the final third.
Let the final section shift energy: warmer, quieter, fuller, bluesier, or more exposed depending on the song family.
End with a memorable human afterimage rather than a generic fade.

participation_patterns: Soft refrain listeners can sing without dense lyrics
Guitar-answer phrase that returns like a second voice
Harmony lift used sparingly for emotional emphasis
Final plainspoken tag that reframes the song
Live-room instrumental break that gives the lyric space
qa_rules: Do not include real artist names in finished lyrics, title, metadata, or audio prompt.
Reject any output that sounds like a direct Craig Cardiff, John Mayer, or Hayden imitation rather than an original adjacent singer-songwriter lane.
Title examples are style references only; do not reuse them unless the user explicitly requests that exact title.
Every song must vary at least three of these: tempo, guitar role, rhythm feel, hook type, vocal delivery, emotional posture, structure, or production density.
If a song is very intimate, confirm it still has a memorable melodic or lyrical hook.
If a song is blues-pop leaning, confirm guitar craft supports the song rather than becoming empty flash.
Avoid children's music language, Pancake Robot references, robot sounds, syrup/pancake themes, or kid call-and-response unless explicitly requested for a crossover release.

vocal_performance_engine.priority: This character's voice earns its place through lived-in grain and conversational intimacy — the sense that every sung line was road-tested in a real room before it was recorded, not polished into smoothness.
vocal_textures: A warm mid-range chest voice with faint roughness at phrase edges, as if the singer has been talking all day and the throat remembers it
Breath-forward delivery on confessional lines, where the air arriving before the vowel is part of the emotional signal
A slight natural crack on isolated high notes that is left in rather than corrected, giving the moment its emotional weight
Hushed near-speech tone in verses when the lyric is at its most specific and personal, dropping back from singing into telling
A fuller, warmer resonance on chorus hooks that widens the chest sound without pushing toward technical power-vocal territory
Subtle regional color in vowel shaping — not an accent, but a slight flatness or drawl on certain words that grounds the voice in a real geography
timing_behaviors: Arrives on the beat slightly behind in verses, leaning into the rhythm from behind like someone who knows where the pocket is and trusts it
Clips final consonants early on emotionally charged words to let the silence carry the weight the lyric just set up
Pushes fractionally ahead of the beat on pre-chorus lines as tension builds, as if the body is leaning into what's coming
Holds extended phrases past the expected breath point on emotionally important lines, using the stretch to signal that this moment costs something
Drops mid-phrase dynamics without warning on the most plainspoken lyric lines, making the ordinary words land harder than the decorated ones
adlib_behaviors: Exhales or near-laughs between lines when the lyric lands somewhere unexpectedly true, not as performance but as acknowledgment
Repeats the final word of a hook line an octave lower or softer as if reconsidering it alone after everyone else has moved on
Hums or la-las a guitar melody during an instrumental break rather than staying silent, keeping the human presence in the arrangement
Inserts a barely-voiced 'yeah' or 'mm' before a verse line when the emotional mood has shifted and the narrator is gathering themselves
Lets a phrase trail into breath rather than a full note on the last line of a final verse, as if the thought is still unfinished when the music ends
avoid: Bright, clean studio-corrected pitch perfection that removes the grain the voice needs to feel like it belongs in a live room
Melisma or sustained note runs that pull focus from the lyric toward vocal display — this voice proves itself through what it says, not how long it can hold a note
Punchy rhythmic vocal performance that flattens the conversational timing and makes the delivery feel like a genre exercise rather than a person
Affecting a deep authoritative baritone gravitas that feels like a posture rather than a natural register
Overshooting the emotional volume on chorus peaks so the vulnerability in the verse is dwarfed and the dynamic arc collapses

performance_conceit_bank: On a tender family-memory song, the vocal drops to near-speech for exactly one line — the one that names the specific person or object — then rises back into melody, as if the real name was almost too private to sing aloud.
On a blues-pop groove track, the voice delays entry by half a bar after the guitar lick opens, letting the string sound make the promise before the narrator shows up to either keep or complicate it.

On a late-night solo acoustic piece, the singer lands on the hook note and then does not sustain it — the syllable cuts off and the guitar string rings the pitch instead, splitting the hook between instrument and voice.

On a road anthem, the chorus doubles the lead vocal with a barely-audible second take that is slightly drier and less confident, creating the impression of the singer in the room and the memory of the singer at the same time.

On an apology or regret song, the narrator drops one lyric line entirely into spoken cadence — no melody, just the plain sentence — and then returns to singing, marking the moment as too honest to dress in tune.

On a fingerpicked folk confession, the vocal phrasing deliberately runs over the bar line on one verse lyric, spilling the sentence past where a tidy arrangement would have contained it, as if the feeling outran the structure.
On any song where the final chorus repeats, the last statement of the title phrase is sung softer than the first, not louder — the resolution arrives by going inward rather than pushing outward.

On a blues-inflected track, the voice bends a syllable in the verse exactly the way the guitar bends a note in the following fill, creating a call-and-response not between two instruments but between the body and the string.

album_mode_lanes.name: Porch Confession
album_mode_lanes.description: Solo or near-solo fingerpicked tracks where the only company is the guitar and the narrator's memory — arrangement is so sparse the listener can hear room tone, and the lyric earns its intimacy by naming one specific irretrievable thing per verse rather than gesturing at loss in general.

album_mode_lanes.name: Blue-Light Groove
album_mode_lanes.description: Relaxed blues-pop songs where the electric guitar is not background texture but a conversational partner — the vocal and the guitar fills complete each other's sentences, and the emotional content is complicated by pleasure rather than uncomplicated by it.

album_mode_lanes.name: Road Company
album_mode_lanes.description: Full-band tracks where the rhythm section creates forward momentum but the lyrics stay grounded in small, specific images rather than big anthem language — the song feels like motion toward something the narrator cannot quite name yet.

album_mode_lanes.name: Dusk Window
album_mode_lanes.description: Slow, low-key indie-folk pieces that do not resolve — tempo sits at the bottom of the range, the mix is dry and close, and the final image is left deliberately ambiguous, trusting the listener to complete the emotional arc in the silence after the track ends.

album_mode_lanes.name: Ordinary Tender
album_mode_lanes.description: Love and devotion songs that earn sentiment through physical specificity rather than elevated language — the emotional peak arrives from noticing a gesture, a habit, or a worn object rather than from stating the feeling directly.

album_mode_lanes.name: Dry Account
album_mode_lanes.description: Character sketches or self-inventories delivered in a more detached, observational voice — the narrator steps slightly outside the feeling and reports it with wry plainness, using humor or understatement as the emotional delivery mechanism rather than vulnerability.

song_differentiation_rules: If the previous track opened with a fingerpicked guitar figure, the next track must open differently: cold vocal, a single electric note, room sound, drum brush, or immediate lyric image without an instrumental intro.

If the previous track resolved with a swelling final chorus, the current track must end smaller: a repeated phrase dropping in volume, a guitar figure alone, an unresolved suspended chord, or a half-spoken trailing line.

If the previous track used a direct first-person love address, the current track must shift perspective to a third-person character sketch, a self-directed inventory, a family scene, or a road observation that does not center romantic feeling.

If the previous track stayed in a slow folk lane, the current track must shift at least two energy variables simultaneously — tempo, guitar texture, and rhythm density — not just increase BPM while keeping everything else the same.

If the previous track's hook was melodically smooth and reassuring, the current track's hook should arrive jagged, plainspoken, or rhythmically interrupted, so the emotional mode of the hook itself signals a different kind of feeling.

If the previous track used brushed drums and warm upright-bass feel, the current track must either remove percussion entirely or introduce a noticeably different rhythmic texture — rim-click groove, more forward kick, or pure acoustic rhythm without kit.
anti_generic_rules: Do not open every song by establishing the narrator alone in a room or vehicle — this character's specific failure mode is the solitary-figure establishing shot, which becomes invisible through repetition and must be interrupted regularly with mid-scene entry or second-person address.

Do not allow plainspoken vocabulary to collapse into bare statement — this character earns emotional weight through one or two precise, unexpected concrete details per song, not through general declarations of longing or love or regret that could belong to any song in this style.

Do not let the blues-pop guitar lane default to a single mood of relaxed cool — this character's electric guitar should carry emotional information, so the tone, dynamics, and note choices must reflect the specific feeling of the song rather than performing a genre aesthetic.

Do not let dry humor become a tic — this character uses wry self-awareness as a release valve at specific moments, not as a continuous vocal personality, and overuse signals that the generator is filling space rather than serving the song.

Do not recycle the energy arc structure as a formula — the ROOM TO HOME LIGHT pattern describes emotional logic, not a required sequence of production moves, and every song must find its own concrete way to move through that logic without re-using the same verse setup, pre-chorus build, or chorus delivery.

Do not resolve emotional ambiguity too cleanly — this character lives in the unfinished, the almost-said, and the quietly unresolved, and a tidy emotional landing on every track flattens what makes the voice distinctive.

Do not treat the guitar as decoration — every arrangement must specify what the guitar is doing emotionally, not just texturally, and if the guitar cannot be described as commenting on the lyric or carrying emotional information, the arrangement must be reconsidered.

Do not allow consecutive songs on an album to share a dominant metaphor cluster — if one song uses weather imagery as its central texture, the next must build its imagery from a different concrete world: body, furniture, clothing, tool, sound, light, or movement.
do_not_repeat_across_album: Do not use a fingerpicked descending arpeggio as the intro figure on more than one track per album — this is the character's most comfortable opening move and must be deliberately rationed.

Do not place the emotional turn in the bridge of three or more consecutive songs — rotate the location of the narrative turn to the second verse, the final chorus, the last couplet of a verse, or the outro.
Do not use road or driving imagery as a framing device in back-to-back tracks — it is one lane of the character's geography, not the character's entire world.

Do not rely on the same verb cluster — arriving, leaving, staying, waiting, remembering — to carry the emotional action across multiple songs; each track must find its own physical or sensory action verb to anchor the lyric.
Do not use the near-speech lyric drop more than once every four tracks — it is a high-impact device that loses power through repetition and must be reserved for the moment it earns the most.
Do not end more than two consecutive songs with a fading repeated hook — vary the ending mode with a single unaccompanied line, a guitar figure alone, a room-sound decay, or a mid-phrase cut.

Do not use hushed close-mic vulnerability as the dominant vocal texture across a whole album — rotate it with more conversational delivery, slight distance, or the fuller chest resonance so the quiet moments remain meaningful by contrast.

Do not use the same seasonal or time-of-day framing — late night, early morning, dusk — in more than two songs on the same album; vary the temporal setting to prevent the album from feeling atmospherically claustrophobic.

Do not place the melodic hook on the same syllable position within the title phrase across multiple tracks — vary whether the emphasis falls on the first word, the last word, or an unexpected middle word so each hook has its own rhythmic identity.
hidden_brief_requirements: The brief must specify a single concrete physical detail unique to this track — not drawn from any other song in this session or album — that anchors the narrator's emotional situation in a specific, irreplaceable object, gesture, or place.

The brief must describe how the electric or acoustic guitar is behaving emotionally on this specific track, not just texturally, including whether it is leading, answering, contradicting, or shadowing the vocal line.
The brief must identify which song-family lane this track occupies and explain what structural or vocal choice will prevent it from sounding like the previous track in the same lane.

The brief must specify the exact emotional posture of the narrator at the end of the song — resolved, unresolved, dryly aware, quietly hopeful, or still mid-feeling — so the arrangement and final image serve that posture rather than defaulting to a generic close.
The brief must state where the narrative or emotional turn falls in this track's structure and confirm it does not fall in the same structural position as the turn in the preceding track.

The brief must specify one performance behavior — a timing choice, an adlib moment, a dynamic drop, or a vocal texture shift — that is unique to this track and has not been used in the same way on any adjacent track in this album sequence.

visuals.style: Warm modern singer-songwriter cover art with porchlight glow, road-map texture, acoustic guitar details, kitchen-window light, handwritten-note grain, small venue poster warmth, and understated editorial composition. Polished enough for streaming artwork; not photorealistic.
visuals.negative_prompt: photorealistic, celebrity likeness, real artist portrait, copied album cover, explicit sex imagery, gore, weapons as focal point, hate symbols, children's cartoon branding, Pancake Robot character, readable copyrighted logos, dense text, messy typography, misspelled words
visuals.text_overlay_style: warm minimal singer-songwriter typography, limited words, paper grain, amber/denim contrast, no dense text blocks

distribution.content_advisory: clean or radio-friendly by default; heartbreak, nostalgia, family complexity, and lightly mature themes allowed without explicit content
